category: decaf mentioned only in a variant name

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty ground-truth labels are supported by the page text or embedded variant data. 'coffee.is_decaf' is supported by the embedded variant name ('Colombia La Serranía Decaf - 250g'). 'coffee.roaster_country' is supported indirectly by 'Oakland, CA' implying United States. 'coffee.is_blend' is supported by the single-origin context ('Colombia', 'Huila', one producer) and listed varieties do not indicate a multi-origin blend. 'coffee.is_espresso' is supported by 'Roast: Filter / Omni' and brew suggestions focused on filter methods rather than espresso. 'coffee.is_coferment_or_infused' is supported insofar as the page states 'Sugarcane EA' decaf process and contains no indication of co-fermentation or infusion.

realism.reasons:
This reads very plausibly like a real specialty coffee product page. The structure is highly believable: storefront header, shipping promo, product title, price, size/grind selectors, tasting notes, origin details, short marketing copy, brew suggestion, related products, variant block, and footer. The coffee details are coherent and industry-appropriate: Huila/Pitalito, Castillo/Caturra, 1,650–1,900 masl, harvest window, Sugarcane EA decaf process, and an omni/filter roast all fit specialty coffee conventions. The tasting notes and descriptive language are natural and not overblown.

The main tell is the mismatch between the visible title and the variant data: the page headline says “Colombia La Serranía,” but the variant names say “Colombia La Serranía Decaf.” That inconsistency is noticeable, especially since Sugarcane EA strongly implies decaf and the body copy never explicitly says decaf. Also, only whole-bean SKUs are shown despite a visible Grind selector, which is slightly suspicious in an html-to-text rendering unless other grind variants were omitted. “default_for_inference” in the variant block is also not something a real storefront would expose to users, though it could come from synthetic extraction metadata rather than the page itself.

Overall, aside from the decaf naming inconsistency and slightly artificial embedded variant formatting, it is close to a real roaster product page.

label.coffee.sensory_text:
brown sugar, pear, cocoa nib

A sweet and easy-drinking Colombian lot from smallholder members of Asociación La Serranía in the hills outside Pitalito. We taste soft pear acidity, brown sugar sweetness, and a clean cocoa finish that makes this a reliable everyday brew across pour-over, batch, and immersion recipes.

The lots for this release were selected for balance and clarity, then prepared using the Sugarcane EA method before roasting gently on our Loring in small batches. Expect a round cup with low bitterness and a mellow finish.
label.coffee.producer_text: A sweet and easy-drinking Colombian lot from smallholder members of Asociación La Serranía in the hills outside Pitalito.
